segments.title: Torpedo boat destroyers grew from torpedo threat to fleet screen
segments.summary: The lecture traces torpedo boat destroyers from ironclad-era torpedo threats through Jeune Ecole imbalance, Royal Navy flotilla defense, and Jutland-scale destroyer use.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke starts with institutional caution and 1860s ironclads, then explains why the Whitehead torpedo created a new delivery problem and why torpedo-boat-only doctrine produced an unbalanced fleet. The later section follows the Royal Navy's move from torpedo boats and torpedo gunboats into Daring, Havoc, and related torpedo boat destroyers: fast ships meant to kill enemy torpedo boats, carry torpedoes themselves, and protect the battle fleet as part of flotilla defense.

evidence.note: The opening frames institutional memory, 1860s ironclads, and the Whitehead torpedo as the technological background for early destroyers.
The lecture moves from HMS Lightning and torpedo delivery into the Jeune Ecole critique, emphasizing the danger of an imbalanced torpedo-boat force.
The first destroyer section explains the Daring, Havoc, Hornet, Decoy, Ferret, and Lynx orders and defines their role in flotilla defense.
The Jutland discussion argues that destroyers and torpedo boats made up most ships present and shaped the battle more than headline capital-ship narratives suggest.
